coredata.dc:title: Regulation of fluorescence emission of carbon dots via hydrogen bonding assembly
coredata.dc:creator: Bai, Jianliang
Yuan, Guojun
Huang, Zhujun
Zhang, Lu
Zhu, Yaqing
Wang, Xinyu
Ren, Lili
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) can be wildly used as fluorescence (FL) sensing, bioimaging, drug delivery and optoelectronic devices because of their distinguished photo-stability against photobleaching, low toxicity and easy surface functionalization toward different applications. However, solid fluorescent CDs are rarely reported due to the notorious aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) effects. Here, we proposed a simple synthesis strategy to prepare CDs via hydrogen bonding self-assembly (HB-CDs). Most important of all, the FL emission of HB-CDs can be easily regulated based on hydrogen bond dynamic adjustability. When hydrogen-bonding break reagents were introduced, the emission shifted from yellow to blue. Hence, we put forward a novel solid-state photoluminescence mechanism, namely, the yellow emission can be excited by the formation of J-aggregates and the blue emission can be excited by dispersion. Based on this phenomenon, we developed a new CDs composites with dual modes FL emissions. These findings may provide a novel strategy to easily prepare CDs with solid-state fluorescence and tunable fluorescence.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hydrogen bonding self-assembly
Solid-state fluorescence
Tunable fluorescence

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The proposed fluorescence principles of (a) Aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) effects. (b) Matrix solid-phase dispersion. (c) Hydrogen bonding cross-linking and breaking.
(a) TEM image and inset (a) the lateral size distribution diagram of CDs. (b) HRTEM image of CDs. (c) SEM image of HB-CDs. (d–f) Mapping images of the HB-CDs.
(a) The FT-IR spectra of MA, CA and HB-CDs. (b) XPS spectra of HB-CDs. (c) The UV–vis absorption spectra of the HB-CDs solution. (d) The UV–vis absorption spectra of the HB-CDs powder.

The FL spectra of (a) pure CDs in H2O. (b) HB-CDs powders. (c) HB-CDs (0.1 g) in DMSO (5 mL). (d) HB-CDs (0.02 g) in DMSO (5 mL). (e) HB-CDs (0.02 g) in DMSO (1 mL) and H2O (4 mL). (f) HB-CDs (0.02 g) in DMSO (1 mL) and CH3OH (4 mL). (g) HB-CDs (0.02 g) in DMSO (1 mL) and acetic acid (4 mL). (h) FL decay curves of HB-CDs powder. (i) Fluorescence decay curves of diluteHB-CDs solution.
Schematic illustration of HB-CDs with different FL emissive states.
HB-CDs formation and yellow emission (left) and blue emission (right) under 365 nm lamp.

head.ce:title: Regulation of fluorescence emission of carbon dots via hydrogen bonding assembly

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) can be wildly used as fluorescence (FL) sensing, bioimaging, drug delivery and optoelectronic devices because of their distinguished photo-stability against photobleaching, low toxicity and easy surface functionalization toward different applications. However, solid fluorescent CDs are rarely reported due to the notorious aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) effects. Here, we proposed a simple synthesis strategy to prepare CDs via hydrogen bonding self-assembly (HB-CDs). Most important of all, the FL emission of HB-CDs can be easily regulated based on hydrogen bond dynamic adjustability. When hydrogen-bonding break reagents were introduced, the emission shifted from yellow to blue. Hence, we put forward a novel solid-state photoluminescence mechanism, namely, the yellow emission can be excited by the formation of J-aggregates and the blue emission can be excited by dispersion. Based on this phenomenon, we developed a new CDs composites with dual modes FL emissions. These findings may provide a novel strategy to easily prepare CDs with solid-state fluorescence and tunable fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of fluorescent nanomaterials, have drawndramatic interests of the scientific researchworkers because of their distinctive advantages such as eco-friendly preparation [1], high photostability [2,3], widely precursor sources [4], low toxicity [5–7]. Extensive applications such as drug delivery, bioimaging, sensor and optoelectronic devices have been achieved on CDs, which mainly due to their unique optical properties [8–13]. However, almost CDs suffer from the fluorescence (FL) quenching in the solid-state due to aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) effects, which is imputed to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and direct π-π interactions, limiting their application in abroaderregion (Fig. 1 a) [8,14–19].

In order to investigate the solid-state FL emission of CDs, many strategies have been reported in recent few years. The mostcommonway is to disperse CDs into a solid matrice (Fig. 1b), for example, high molecular polymers (poly(vinyl alcohol, polyacid, polyurethane) [19–22], starch [17,24], mesoporous materials (aluminas and silica) [25–27], inorganic salts (such as NaCl, KCl, KBr and BaSO4) [28–30], agar [31] and cellulose nanofiber [32]. Since the matrices act as the role of media like solvents, the fluorescence of these CDs can be maintained through uniform spread and space limitation. However, the previously reported methods often involve complicated synthesis process and high preparation cost [18,31]. And even worse, if too many CDs are introduced to solid matrices, the ACQ effect will appear because of the accumulation of CDs due to the limited space of the matrix [33]. What’s more, the mechanism of solid-state CDs with FL is still not clear because few studies have reported on the interaction between CDs and matrices. Hence, in order to explore the luminescence mechanism of solid-state CDs, exploring a new type of CDs-based fluorescence materials with a clear structure and obvious resistance to ACQ effects is necessary.

Recently, our groups reported two solid-state CDs by changing combination sequence between metal organic frameworks (MOFs) and CDs, which showed red-shift or blue-shift for the FL emission [34]. While, the CDs-based fluorescent MOFs is toxic and complex to prepare, which greatly limits their practical application. Thus, there is an urgentneed for metal-free CDs-based fluorescence materials which can be prepared via a low-cost synthesis way. Herein, we designed a new type of CDs-based composite with hydrogen bonding self-assembled (HB-CDs), which can be easily prepared through heating the precursors of citric acid monohydrate (CA) and melamine (MA) in acetic acid, just as shown in Scheme 1 . It should be noted that MA alone, as a precursor, cannot form carbon dots through solvent thermal reaction and can only serve as a good hydrogen bond donor and acceptor (Fig. S1 and S2). Our HB-CDs exhibit yellow solid-state FL emission under 365 nm UV lamp. However, the emission shifts from yellow to blue gradually when hydrogen-bonding break inductors are introduced, finally showing similar FL characteristics as pure CDs (Fig. 1c). The structure of the HB-CDs was investigated and the solid-state luminescence mechanism were proposed. These dual modes fluorescence emission behavior, attributeto the existence of J-aggregated and dispersed HB-CDs. In this report, a whole new strategy was proposed, which offered a fascinating compass to avoid self-quenching phenomenon and tune of fluorescence CDs.

The HB-CDs was synthesized by a two-step hydrothermal approach using CA and MA as precursor, and final yellow powders were acquiredviarotary evaporation and vacuum drying. Hydrogen bonds act as crosslinkers among them. The HB-CDs can emit yellow and blue FL corresponding to Aggregation and Dispersion (Scheme 1). Moreimportantly, the solid-state CDs absent dual mode fluorescence emission behaviors among the reported CDs-based materials so far [17,19–32]. It should be noted that the obtained HB-CDs possesses different modes of luminescence corresponding to different degrees of hydrogen-bond inducedatmosphere.

In order to explore the “dual modes fluorescence” feature of the HB-CDs materials, themorphologiesandparticle sizes of pure CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM), respectively. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the TEM image revealed that pure CDs exhibited well dispersed quasi-spherical shapes with an average size of 2.57 nm. In addition, the HRTEM image indicates that the CDs have a clear crystalline lattice fringe with a spacing of 2.3 nm, which are similar to the crystal plane of graphene (100) plane (Fig. 2b) [35]. As displayed by the SEM, the HB-CDs composites are constructed with coralline particles. The image of HB-CDs shows its multilayer hollow structure (Fig. 2c). In order to prove such structure, the elemental mapping of HB-CDs was adopted (Fig. 2d–f). Because only MA has N element, the N element map proves that the distribution of MA is uniform, so FL quenching of CDs can be effectively avoided.

As seen in Fig. 3 a, the FT-IR spectra of the HB-CDs and precursors were obtained. The HB-CDs (black line) displayed broad absorptions from approximately 2876 to 3675 cm−1, attributed to the stretching vibrations of NH2, OH and CH2 groups [36]. The peaks located at 1724, 1655 and 1544 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of CO, the bending vibration of N-H and the stretching vibration of CN, respectively [37,38]. The broad absorption band from approximately 967 to 1304 cm−1 should be caused by CN and CO vibrations [36,38]. Besides, the aromatic triazine ring stretching vibration of MA appeared at 1436 cm−1 [39,40]. It is well known that MA is an excellent candidate for hydrogen-bonding aggregates [40]. These results imply that melamine and CDs may form self-assembled aggregates via hydrogen-bonding.

In order to further verify the above FT-IR analyses, X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of the HB-CDs was measured. As shown in Fig. 3b, Fig. S3 and Table S1, the HB-CDs mainly include C, N and O elements with percentage to be 46.32%, 15.31% and 38.37%, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum exhibits three main peaks at 284.5, 285.2 and 288.2 eV, which were attributed to CC/CC, CO/CN and CO/CN bonds in the HB-CDs (Fig. S3a) [40,41]. The typical peaks at 399.1 eV and 400.7 eV are assigned to CNC and NH, respectively (Fig. S3b) [40,41]. The XPS O 1s spectrum is deconvoluted into three peaks at 531.3, 532.5 and 532.9 eV, contributing to CO, CO/OH and OCO bonds, respectively (Fig. S3c) [42]. Both FT-IR and XPS results confirm the large numbers of NH2 and OH groups exist on the surface of HB-CDs, which can effectively form intermolecular hydrogen bonds.

Asiswell-known, a red-shift in the absorption spectra can be used to characterize the J-aggregates of dyes [43,44]. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the as prepared HB-CDs powder and solution were measured to analyze their optical properties. As seen in Fig. 3c, The HB-CDs solution exhibits strong absorption bands around 342 nm, which is originated from both π-π* transition and n-π* transition related to oxygen or nitrogen containing edge groups [45–47]. For comparison, the HB-CDs powders exhibit a different broader absorption owing to the hydrogen bonding between the CDs and MA (Fig. 3d) [48]. Similar to previous studies [49,50], this red-shifted phenomenon of HB-CDs hints that CDs and MA may form J-aggregates in powder conditions.

Thus, the dual modes FL emission behavior from the HB-CDs may be proposed preliminarily as follows. The HB-CDspowder is freely soluble in DMSO solution. Pure CDs showed obvious excitation-dependent emissions [51,52]. As the excitation wavelength increased from 320 to 460 nm, the FL emission peaks were red-shifted at the same time (Fig. 4 a). However, excitation wavelength increased from 320 to 460 nm, the emission maxima were fixed at almost same position (about 523 nm) for both solid and high concentration DMSO solution (Fig. 4b and c). And it is well known that excitation-independent FL is one of characteristics of molecular fluorophores [53,54], which hints that FL origin of our HB-CDs powders or CDs with high concentration in DMSO solution are the molecular-state. We find that the HB-CDs will show blue emission under 365 nm excitation wavelength in protic donor or acceptor solvent, such as low concentration DMSO solution, H2O, CH3OH and acetic acid (Fig. 4d–g). These similar spectroscopic results demonstrate that those solutions possess a similar FL origin to pure CDs solution [55]. In addition, the HB-CDs synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal approach using CA and MA as precursors, shows a similar spectra with that synthesized by a two-step method (Fig. S4). To further understand the origin of FL, we measured FL lifetimes of HB-CDs powder and low concentration DMSO solution of HB-CDs (Fig. 4h and i). They have two different average lifetimes of 3.04 ns for HB-CDs powders and 10.53 ns for HB-CDs dilute solution (Table S2). The different FL lifetimes indicate that they have different FL origins.

By performing spectroscopic analysis and comparing HB-CDs in solid or in dilute solution. As summarized in Fig. 5 , HB-CDs powder has a single FL peak originated from the molecular-state fluorescence. For HB-CDs powder, strong hydrogen bonding interactions were constructed between CDs and MA, driving the building blocks to assemble in a head-to-tail mode (Fig. 5a and Scheme 1). Then, the CDs were incorporated into network constructing HB-CDs through the hydrogen bonding interactions, forming J-aggregates structure. Incontrast, in dilute solution, CDs synthesized at high temperature which resulted in high carbonization, have multiple FL origins that are caused by various core-states [42,54]. The core-states originate from different size of sp2 domain, so they have various energy levels showing excitation-dependent FL (Fig. 5b).

In summary, we developed a facile method to prepare metal-free solid-state fluorescent material by directly constructing carbon dots-hydrogen bonding self-assembled aggregates (HB-CDs) through hydrothermal synthesis strategy with MA and CA as the precursor, hydrogen bonds act as crosslinkers among them. As far as we know, this is the first report of solid-state CDs have “dual modes fluorescence” feature via hydrogen bonding interactions. Further studies reveal that CDs and MA can form J-aggregates. Molecular-state constructed by hydrogen bond networks is main FL origin of HB-CDs powders. On the contrary, because of hydrogen bonds have been broken, the core-states are main FL origins of HB-CDs dilute solution. This study may not only provide a new strategy to prepare solid-state fluorescent material, but also expand the tunable fluorescence methods.

Jianliang Bai: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Guojun Yuan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Zhujun Huang: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Lu Zhang: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Yaqing Zhu: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Xinyu Wang: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Lili Ren: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge A Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (1107047002) and Innovation platform project supported by Jiangsu Province (6907041203).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2021.108500.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-​color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Jiang, S. Sun, L. Zhang, Y. Lu, A. G. Wu, C. Z. Cai, H. W. Lin, Red, Green, and Blue Luminescence by Carbon Dots: Full-​Color Emission Tuning and Multicolor Cellular Imaging, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 54 (2015) 5360-5363.
